requirements.title: Before you start
requirements.description:
**Required before setup**

- A **price forecast** source that covers the full plan period
- Forecast values as timestamp/value pairs (`start`, `value`)

**Optional**

- A usage forecast source
- A PV forecast source
- An export price forecast source when PV is configured

You can either reference an existing entity that already exposes forecast data in its attributes, or write a Jinja2 template that returns the expected timestamp/value list.

**Unsure what to do?**
See the example walkthrough for a complete setup path using Strømligning, Deye, and Solcast:
[Open the example guide on GitHub](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/example-deye-solcast-stromligning.md).

planner_setup.title: Add new WattPlan setup
planner_setup.description: Create one WattPlan setup for your home.

data.name: Setup name
data.hours_to_plan: How many hours ahead should the plan cover

data_description.name: This name will identify this WattPlan setup in Home Assistant.
data_description.slot_minutes: This is the plan resolution. A lower value means more detailed plans and more required forecast values.
data_description.hours_to_plan: WattPlan needs forecasts that cover the full selected period.

setup_complete.title: Setup complete
setup_complete.description:
You have now set up the **{setup_name}** WattPlan planner.

Current planner settings:
- Resolution: **{slot_minutes} minutes**
- Planning horizon: **{plan_hours} hours**
- Price source: **{price_source}**
- Usage source: **{usage_source}**
- PV source: **{pv_source}**
- Export price source: **{export_price_source}**

WattPlan will now begin producing plans on its schedule and after relevant setup changes.

**Does this work?** WattPlan continuously pulls the configured forecast data, updates its plan, and exposes the current integration health through the `status` sensors. If something goes wrong, degraded and failed states are shown there and actionable source issues appear in Home Assistant Repairs. [Read more about error handling](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/error-handling.md)

Planning and optimization are only part of the setup. To really benefit, WattPlan needs something that can shift load away from expensive periods and into cheaper ones.

Good next steps:
- Add a **battery** if you want WattPlan to plan charging and discharging
- Add **optional loads** such as a washing machine if you want WattPlan to suggest when people should start them
- Add **comfort loads** if you want WattPlan to shift flexible always-needed loads
- Create automations that translate WattPlan action entities into real device commands

In practice, batteries, optional loads, and comfort loads are the main ways to turn plans into savings.

Click **Finish** to complete the integration setup and give the new planner device a name and/or area.

source_price.title: Price source
source_price.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

**Choose how WattPlan reads your price forecast**

This source is specifically the cost of buying power from the grid, so it should reflect your real import price.

1. **Entity attribute** (preferred): Read a forecast list directly from an entity attribute.
2. **Service call** (preferred): Call a Home Assistant service that returns a forecast list.
3. **Template**: Use Jinja2 to build a forecast list from one or more entities.

Each source type must produce enough forecast data for the planner window.

data_description.source_mode: Choose how to provide your price forecast. Entity attribute and Service call are the preferred setup paths for price sources. Template is the advanced fallback when you need to reshape data yourself.

source_price_template.title: Price source template
source_price_template.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

Provide a template that returns price forecast data.

Return either:
- a list of objects with `start` and `value`
- or a list of numeric values

Target intervals for this setup: {required_count} values at {slot_minutes}-minute intervals.

data_description.template: Return a native Python-style list from Jinja, not a JSON string.
data_description.fixup_profile: Direct only uses only clean input. Repair local gaps also fills small gaps near known values. Extend daily pattern can also repeat values from 24 hours earlier when enough data already exists.

data_description.aggregation_mode: This always applies when multiple source samples land in one planner interval.
data_description.clamp_mode: Nearest interval snaps timestamps to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval. Strict requires exact interval timestamps.
data_description.resample_mode: Linear interpolates between nearby known values. Forward fill repeats the most recent value across short gaps.
data_description.edge_fill_mode: Hold edge repeats the nearest known value at the beginning or end when the source is almost complete.

source_price_adapter.title: Price source entity attribute
source_price_adapter.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

Use an entity attribute for price forecast data.

Adapter type formats:
- **Auto detect** (recommended): inspect one or more entities and infer the list path plus timestamp/value keys
- **Attribute objects**: use an explicit list of objects with timestamp/value keys
- **Attribute values**: use an explicit list of numeric values

data_description.entity_id: One or more entities to inspect. Auto detect checks them in order and uses the first compatible forecast list it finds.
data_description.adapter_type: Choose Auto detect for the recommended setup path, or choose an explicit adapter type to enter the fields yourself.
data_description.fixup_profile: Direct only uses only clean input. Repair local gaps also fills small gaps near known values. Extend daily pattern can also repeat values from 24 hours earlier when enough data already exists.

manual.name: Manual mapping
manual.description: Only applies when "Auto detect" is not selected.

data_description.name: Attribute path that contains the forecast list. Used only when Auto detect is not selected.
data_description.time_key: Key containing the timestamp. Used only when Auto detect is not selected.
data_description.value_key: Key containing the numeric value. Used only when Auto detect is not selected.

data_description.aggregation_mode: This always applies when multiple source samples land in one planner interval.
data_description.clamp_mode: Nearest interval snaps timestamps to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval. Strict requires exact interval timestamps.
data_description.resample_mode: Linear interpolates between nearby known values. Forward fill repeats the most recent value across short gaps.
data_description.edge_fill_mode: Hold edge repeats the nearest known value at the beginning or end when the source is almost complete.

source_price_service.title: Price source service call
source_price_service.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

Use a Home Assistant service call for price forecast data.

Adapter type formats:
- **Auto detect** (recommended): inspect the returned payload and infer the list path plus timestamp/value keys
- **Service call**: use an explicit response path and key mapping

Services must not require input arguments.

data_description.service: Service to call in `domain.service` format. WattPlan calls it without arguments and reads the returned response.
data_description.adapter_type: Choose Auto detect for the recommended setup path, or Service call to enter the fields yourself.
data_description.fixup_profile: Direct only uses only clean input. Repair local gaps also fills small gaps near known values. Extend daily pattern can also repeat values from 24 hours earlier when enough data already exists.

manual.name: Manual mapping
manual.description: Only applies when "Auto detect" is not selected.

data_description.name: Root path containing the forecast list in the service response. Used only when Auto detect is not selected.
data_description.time_key: Key containing the timestamp. Used only when Auto detect is not selected.
data_description.value_key: Key containing the numeric value. Used only when Auto detect is not selected.

data_description.aggregation_mode: This always applies when multiple source samples land in one planner interval.
data_description.clamp_mode: Nearest interval snaps timestamps to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval. Strict requires exact interval timestamps.
data_description.resample_mode: Linear interpolates between nearby known values. Forward fill repeats the most recent value across short gaps.
data_description.edge_fill_mode: Hold edge repeats the nearest known value at the beginning or end when the source is almost complete.

source_export_price.title: Export price source
source_export_price.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

**Choose how WattPlan reads your export price forecast**

1. **Entity attribute** (preferred): Read a forecast list directly from an entity attribute.
2. **Service call** (preferred): Call a Home Assistant service that returns a forecast list.
3. **Template**: Use Jinja2 to build a forecast list from one or more entities.
4. **Not used**: Treat exported power as having zero value.

You can set up this source later from the integration page. To **skip** this setup now, pick **Not used**.

If used, the source must produce enough forecast data for the planner window.

data_description.source_mode: Choose how to provide your export price forecast. Entity attribute and Service call are the preferred setup paths. Template is the advanced fallback. Not used values exported power at zero.

source_usage.title: Usage source
source_usage.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

**Choose how WattPlan reads your usage forecast**

1. **Built in** (preferred): Build a forecast from historical load in Home Assistant.
2. **Entity attribute**: Read a forecast list directly from an entity attribute.
3. **Service call**: Call a Home Assistant service that returns a forecast list.
4. **Template**: Use Jinja2 to build a forecast list from one or more entities.
5. **Not used**: Skip usage forecasting for this setup.

You can set up this source later from the integration page. To **skip** this setup now, pick **Not used**.

If used, the source must produce enough forecast data for the planner window.

data_description.source_mode: Choose how to provide your usage forecast. Built in is the preferred setup path for load forecasting. Entity attribute and Service call can reuse existing forecast data. Template is the advanced fallback. Not used skips usage.

source_usage_built_in.title: Usage source built in forecast
source_usage_built_in.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

Use Home Assistant history to forecast usage for the next planner window.

Provide a sensor with your running usage in kWh. WattPlan uses recorder history and long-term statistics from the last N days, with weekday weighting, to build enough values for your selected {slot_minutes}-minute interval.

data_description.entity_id: Sensor that represents current/observed load in kWh. This sensor should update regularly.
data_description.history_days: How many days of past data to use. More days are smoother; fewer days react faster to recent changes.

source_pv.title: PV source
source_pv.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

**Choose how WattPlan reads your PV forecast**

1. **Energy provider** (preferred when applicable): Use a Home Assistant integration that exposes an Energy solar forecast.
2. **Entity attribute**: Read a forecast list directly from an entity attribute.
3. **Service call**: Call a Home Assistant service that returns a forecast list.
4. **Template**: Use Jinja2 to build a forecast list from one or more entities.
5. **Not used**: Skip PV forecasting for this setup.

You can set up this source later from the integration page. To **skip** this setup now, pick **Not used**.

If used, the source must produce enough forecast data for the planner window.

data_description.source_mode: Choose how to provide your PV forecast. Energy provider is the preferred setup path for solar forecasts. Entity attribute and Service call can reuse existing forecast data. Template is the advanced fallback. Not used skips PV.

source_pv_energy_provider.title: PV source Energy provider
source_pv_energy_provider.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

Use a Home Assistant Energy solar forecast provider for PV data.

Select a loaded integration that exposes Energy solar forecast data. WattPlan converts the provider's hourly Wh forecast into kWh planner intervals and extends the horizon by repeating the value from 24 hours earlier.

Target intervals for this setup: {required_count} values at {slot_minutes}-minute intervals.

data_description.config_entry_id: Choose the Home Assistant integration entry that should provide the solar forecast.
data_description.fixup_profile: Energy provider mode always extends the horizon by repeating the value from 24 hours earlier.

manual.name: Manual mapping
manual.description: Only applies when "Auto detect" is not selected.

data_description.aggregation_mode: This always applies when multiple source samples land in one planner interval.
data_description.clamp_mode: Nearest interval snaps timestamps to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval. Strict requires exact interval timestamps.
data_description.resample_mode: Linear interpolates between nearby known values. Forward fill repeats the most recent value across short gaps.
data_description.edge_fill_mode: Hold edge stays enabled in Energy provider mode so the initial daily forecast can always be expanded to the full horizon.

source_review.title: Review source coverage
source_review.description:
**Raw source coverage**

- The source itself provided **{raw_coverage_summary}**
- Raw coverage starts at **{raw_coverage_start}**
- Raw coverage ends at **{raw_coverage_end}**

**Adjusted planner coverage**

- After WattPlan repair/fill, the planner has **{adjusted_coverage_summary}**
- Adjusted coverage starts at **{adjusted_coverage_start}**
- Adjusted coverage ends at **{adjusted_coverage_end}**

**Review**

{review_text}

{diagnostic_text}

{accept_note}

data_description.accept_source_summary: Enable this to continue. Leave it off to return to the source input page and adjust the provider or fixup settings.

error.text_too_long: That is too long. Please shorten it.
error.template_required: A template is required for template mode.
error.template_invalid_json: Template output is not valid JSON. Render a JSON list of forecast entries before submitting.
error.adapter_config_required: Entity and adapter type are required for adapter mode.
error.entity_not_found: The selected entity was not found.
error.built_in_requires_energy_kwh: Select an energy sensor with unit `kWh` for the built-in usage forecast.
error.attribute_name_required: An attribute name is required for this adapter.
error.invalid_payload: Source output must be a list of numeric values or a list of objects containing timestamp and value fields.
error.auto_detect_no_match: Auto detect did not find a compatible forecast list. Review the candidate paths and pick manual mapping if needed.
error.auto_detect_conflict: Auto detect found forecast data, but the selected entities did not resolve to the same root path and fields.
error.built_in_no_numeric_history: No usable numeric history was found for the selected load sensor in the configured lookback period.
error.review_ready: This source is **ready** for the selected horizon. ✅
error.review_ready_extended: This source is usable for the selected horizon, but only **{raw_coverage_summary}** came from the source itself. WattPlan repaired or extended it to cover the full plan. ⚠️ If you can provide more native forecast data, go back and choose a source with longer coverage.
error.review_limited_history: This source is usable, but only {history_days} days of history were found. Forecast quality may be poor.
error.review_incomplete: This source does not cover the full planning horizon. ❌ Choose a fixup profile to repair gaps or extend the tail.
error.review_has_gaps: This source has gaps between known samples. ❌ A fixup profile is recommended.
error.review_has_gaps_repaired: This source has gaps between known samples, but the selected fixup profile will repair them for planning. ✅
error.review_accept_note_valid: Acceptance is already enabled because this source meets the current requirements. Disable it to return and adjust the input.
error.review_accept_note_invalid: Next will bring you back to the source input page so you can adjust the provider or fixup settings.
error.invalid_start: One or more entries has an invalid timestamp. Check the configured time key and use ISO format with timezone.
error.invalid_value: One or more entries has a non-numeric value. Check the configured value key and output numeric values.
error.not_enough_values: The source produced {available_count} usable values for {slot_minutes}-minute intervals, but {required_count} are needed. Try enabling timestamp alignment, aggregation, resampling, or edge fill to produce enough values for the selected horizon.
error.template_invalid_structure: Template returned text. Return a native list of objects or numeric values.
error.source_fetch_error: Source data could not be fetched. Check entity/template settings and try again.
error.energy_provider_none_available: No loaded Home Assistant Energy solar forecast providers were found.
error.energy_provider_unavailable: The selected Energy solar forecast provider is unavailable or returned no forecast data.

init.title: Planner settings
init.description: Edit planner settings and forecast sources for this setup.

planner_core.title: Core settings
planner_core.description: Set interval size and planning horizon. These values define how many forecast values are required.

data.hours_to_plan: How many hours ahead should the plan cover

data_description.slot_minutes: This is the plan resolution. A lower value means more detailed plans and more required forecast values.
data_description.hours_to_plan: WattPlan needs forecasts that cover the full selected period.

source_price.title: Price source
source_price.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

**Choose how WattPlan reads your price forecast**

This source is specifically the cost of buying power from the grid, so it should reflect your real import price.

1. **Entity attribute** (preferred): Read a forecast list directly from an entity attribute.
2. **Service call** (preferred): Call a Home Assistant service that returns a forecast list.
3. **Template**: Use Jinja2 to build a forecast list from one or more entities.

Each source type must produce enough forecast data for the planner window.

data_description.source_mode: Choose how to provide your price forecast. Entity attribute and Service call are the preferred setup paths for price sources. Template is the advanced fallback when you need to reshape data yourself.

source_price_template.title: Price source template
source_price_template.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

Provide a template that returns price forecast data.

Return either:
- a list of objects with `start` and `value`
- or a list of numeric values

Target intervals for this setup: {required_count} values at {slot_minutes}-minute intervals.

data_description.template: Return a native Python-style list from Jinja, not a JSON string.
data_description.fixup_profile: Direct only uses only clean input. Repair local gaps also fills small gaps near known values. Extend daily pattern can also repeat values from 24 hours earlier when enough data already exists.

manual.name: Manual mapping
manual.description: Only applies when "Auto detect" is not selected.

data_description.aggregation_mode: This always applies when multiple source samples land in one planner interval.
data_description.clamp_mode: Nearest interval snaps timestamps to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval. Strict requires exact interval timestamps.
data_description.resample_mode: Linear interpolates between nearby known values. Forward fill repeats the most recent value across short gaps.
data_description.edge_fill_mode: Hold edge repeats the nearest known value at the beginning or end when the source is almost complete.

source_price_adapter.title: Price source entity attribute
source_price_adapter.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

Use an entity attribute for price forecast data.

Adapter type formats:
- **Auto detect** (recommended): inspect one or more entities and infer the list path plus timestamp/value keys
- **Attribute objects**: use an explicit list of objects with timestamp/value keys
- **Attribute values**: use an explicit list of numeric values

data_description.entity_id: One or more entities to inspect. Auto detect checks them in order and uses the first compatible forecast list it finds.
data_description.adapter_type: Choose Auto detect for the recommended setup path, or choose an explicit adapter type to enter the fields yourself.
data_description.name: Attribute path that contains the forecast list. Ignored in auto detect mode.
data_description.time_key: Key containing the timestamp. Ignored in auto detect mode.
data_description.value_key: Key containing the numeric value. Ignored in auto detect mode.
data_description.fixup_profile: Direct only uses only clean input. Repair local gaps also fills small gaps near known values. Extend daily pattern can also repeat values from 24 hours earlier when enough data already exists.

manual.name: Manual mapping
manual.description: Only applies when "Auto detect" is not selected.

data_description.aggregation_mode: This always applies when multiple source samples land in one planner interval.
data_description.clamp_mode: Nearest interval snaps timestamps to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval. Strict requires exact interval timestamps.
data_description.resample_mode: Linear interpolates between nearby known values. Forward fill repeats the most recent value across short gaps.
data_description.edge_fill_mode: Hold edge repeats the nearest known value at the beginning or end when the source is almost complete.

source_export_price.title: Export price source
source_export_price.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

**Choose how WattPlan reads your export price forecast**

1. **Entity attribute** (preferred): Read a forecast list directly from an entity attribute.
2. **Service call** (preferred): Call a Home Assistant service that returns a forecast list.
3. **Template**: Use Jinja2 to build a forecast list from one or more entities.
4. **Not used**: Treat exported power as having zero value.

You can set up this source later from the integration page. To **skip** this setup now, pick **Not used**.

If used, the source must produce enough forecast data for the planner window.

data_description.source_mode: Choose how to provide your export price forecast. Entity attribute and Service call are the preferred setup paths. Template is the advanced fallback. Not used values exported power at zero.

source_usage.title: Usage source
source_usage.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

**Choose how WattPlan reads your usage forecast**

1. **Built in** (preferred): Build a forecast from historical load in Home Assistant.
2. **Entity attribute**: Read a forecast list directly from an entity attribute.
3. **Service call**: Call a Home Assistant service that returns a forecast list.
4. **Template**: Use Jinja2 to build a forecast list from one or more entities.
5. **Not used**: Skip usage forecasting for this setup.

You can set up this source later from the integration page. To **skip** this setup now, pick **Not used**.

If used, the source must produce enough forecast data for the planner window.

data_description.source_mode: Choose how to provide your usage forecast. Built in is the preferred setup path for load forecasting. Entity attribute and Service call can reuse existing forecast data. Template is the advanced fallback. Not used skips usage.

source_usage_built_in.title: Usage source built in forecast
source_usage_built_in.description:
Use Home Assistant history to forecast usage for the next planner window.

Provide a sensor with your running usage in kWh. WattPlan uses recorder history and long-term statistics from the last N days, with weekday weighting, to build enough values for your selected {slot_minutes}-minute interval.

data_description.entity_id: Sensor that represents current/observed load in kWh. This sensor should update regularly.
data_description.history_days: How many days of past data to use. More days are smoother; fewer days react faster to recent changes.

source_usage_template.title: Usage source template
source_usage_template.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

Provide a template that returns usage forecast data.

Return either:
- a list of objects with `start` and `value`

data_description.aggregation_mode: This always applies when multiple source samples land in one planner interval.
data_description.clamp_mode: Nearest interval snaps timestamps to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval. Strict requires exact interval timestamps.
data_description.resample_mode: Linear interpolates between nearby known values. Forward fill repeats the most recent value across short gaps.

source_pv.title: PV source
source_pv.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

**Choose how WattPlan reads your PV forecast**

1. **Energy provider** (preferred when applicable): Use a Home Assistant integration that exposes an Energy solar forecast.
2. **Entity attribute**: Read a forecast list directly from an entity attribute.
3. **Service call**: Call a Home Assistant service that returns a forecast list.
4. **Template**: Use Jinja2 to build a forecast list from one or more entities.
5. **Not used**: Skip PV forecasting for this setup.

You can set up this source later from the integration page. To **skip** this setup now, pick **Not used**.

If used, the source must produce enough forecast data for the planner window.

data_description.source_mode: Choose how to provide your PV forecast. Energy provider is the preferred setup path for solar forecasts. Entity attribute and Service call can reuse existing forecast data. Template is the advanced fallback. Not used skips PV.

source_pv_template.title: PV source template
source_pv_template.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

Provide a template that returns PV forecast data.

Return either:
- a list of objects with `start` and `value`

data_description.adapter_type: Choose Auto detect for the recommended setup path, or choose an explicit adapter type to enter the fields yourself.

data_description.value_key: Key containing the numeric value. Ignored in auto detect mode.

data_description.edge_fill_mode: Hold edge repeats the nearest known value at the beginning or end when the source is almost complete.

source_pv_energy_provider.title: PV source Energy provider
source_pv_energy_provider.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

Use a Home Assistant Energy solar forecast provider for PV data.

Select a loaded integration that exposes Energy solar forecast data. WattPlan converts the provider's hourly Wh forecast into kWh planner intervals and extends the horizon by repeating the value from 24 hours earlier.

Target intervals for this setup: {required_count} values at {slot_minutes}-minute intervals.

data_description.config_entry_id: Choose the Home Assistant integration entry that should provide the solar forecast.
data_description.fixup_profile: Energy provider mode always extends the horizon by repeating the value from 24 hours earlier.

data_description.aggregation_mode: This always applies when multiple source samples land in one planner interval.
data_description.clamp_mode: Nearest interval snaps timestamps to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval. Strict requires exact interval timestamps.
data_description.resample_mode: Linear interpolates between nearby known values. Forward fill repeats the most recent value across short gaps.
data_description.edge_fill_mode: Hold edge stays enabled in Energy provider mode so the initial daily forecast can always be expanded to the full horizon.

source_review.title: Review source coverage
source_review.description:
**Raw source coverage**

- The source itself provided **{raw_coverage_summary}**
- Raw coverage starts at **{raw_coverage_start}**
- Raw coverage ends at **{raw_coverage_end}**

**Adjusted planner coverage**

- After WattPlan repair/fill, the planner has **{adjusted_coverage_summary}**
- Adjusted coverage starts at **{adjusted_coverage_start}**
- Adjusted coverage ends at **{adjusted_coverage_end}**

**Review**

{review_text}

{diagnostic_text}

{accept_note}

data_description.accept_source_summary: Enable this to continue. Leave it off to return to the source input page and adjust the provider or fixup settings.

planner_timers.title: Planner timers
planner_timers.description: When enabled, planning and action publishing run every {slot_minutes} minutes.

data_description.planning_enabled: When enabled, optimization runs every {slot_minutes} minutes and publishes the new plan to WattPlan entities.
data_description.action_emission_enabled: When enabled, action states like charge/discharge/hold are published every {slot_minutes} minutes.
data_description.optimizer_profile:
Choose how actively WattPlan should use batteries.

- Aggressive: Favors savings and accepts more battery movement.
- Balanced: Good default for most homes; balances savings and calmer battery behavior.
- Conservative: Avoids smaller moves and keeps battery behavior steadier.

[Read more here](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/optimizer-profiles.md).

data_description.item_id: Select the battery configuration you want to edit.

data_description.name: A friendly name for this battery in WattPlan entities and services.
data_description.soc_source: Entity that reports current battery state of charge.
data_description.capacity_kwh: Total usable battery capacity in kilowatt-hours.
data_description.minimum_kwh: Lowest allowed energy level for planning. For lithium batteries, a good starting point is about 10% of capacity, for example 1 kWh on a 10 kWh battery.
data_description.max_charge_kw: Maximum charging power the battery can accept. A reasonable starting point is about half the battery capacity in kW, for example 5 kW on a 10 kWh battery.
data_description.max_discharge_kw: Maximum discharging power the battery can provide. A reasonable starting point is about half the battery capacity in kW, for example 5 kW on a 10 kWh battery.
data_description.can_charge_from_grid: Enable if this battery can charge from grid energy prices.
data_description.can_charge_from_pv: Enable if this battery can be charged from PV production.

data_description.item_id: Select the battery configuration you want to remove.

comfort_entities.title: Comfort entities
comfort_entities.description: Edit or remove configured comfort loads.

data_description.item_id: Select the comfort load configuration you want to edit.

data_description.rolling_window_hours: The rolling period used for on-time accounting. Example: 24 means target on-time is evaluated over the latest 24 hours.
data_description.target_on_hours_per_window: How many hours this load should be on within each rolling window. This must be less than or equal to window duration.
data_description.min_consecutive_on_minutes: Once turned on, keep it on for at least this long. Values are rounded to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval.
data_description.min_consecutive_off_minutes: Once turned off, keep it off for at least this long. Values are rounded to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval.
data_description.max_consecutive_off_minutes: If off for longer than this, planning will force the load back on. Values are rounded to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval.
data_description.on_off_source: Binary sensor used to determine whether the load is currently on or off. WattPlan also uses its history to synthesize runtime context.
data_description.expected_power_kw: Nominal power usage while this load is on.
data_description.measured_power_source: Optional sensor for actual power draw. If provided, future planning can adapt using measured behavior.
data_description.name: A friendly name for this comfort load in WattPlan entities and services.

comfort_remove_select.title: Remove comfort load
comfort_remove_select.description: Select which comfort load to remove.

data_description.item_id: Select the comfort load configuration you want to remove.

optional_entities.title: Optional entities
optional_entities.description: Edit or remove configured optional loads.

data_description.item_id: Select the optional load configuration you want to edit.

data_description.run_within_hours: The planner schedules starts between now and this many hours ahead.
data_description.energy_kwh: Total energy needed for one run.
data_description.options: How many start-time options to return. Each option uses a full duration block and options cannot overlap.
data_description.min_option_gap_minutes: Minimum time between option start times. Larger gaps reduce how many non-overlapping options can fit.
data_description.name: A friendly name for this optional load in WattPlan entities and services.

optional_remove_select.title: Remove optional load
optional_remove_select.description: Select which optional load to remove.

data_description.item_id: Select the optional load configuration you want to remove.

source_price_service.description:
Questions about source setup? See the source guide on GitHub: [Open source guide](https://github.com/LordMike/WattPlan/blob/main/docs/source-data.md).

Use a Home Assistant service call for price forecast data.

Adapter type formats:
- **Auto detect** (recommended): inspect the returned payload and infer the list path plus timestamp/value keys
- **Service call**: use an explicit response path and key mapping

Services must not require input arguments.

- **Service call**: use an explicit response path and key mapping

Services must not require input arguments.

data_description.service: Service to call in `domain.service` format. WattPlan calls it without arguments and reads the returned response.
data_description.adapter_type: Choose Auto detect for the recommended setup path, or Service call to enter the fields yourself.
data_description.name: Root path containing the forecast list in the service response. Ignored in auto detect mode.
data_description.time_key: Key containing the timestamp. Ignored in auto detect mode.
data_description.value_key: Key containing the numeric value. Ignored in auto detect mode.
data_description.fixup_profile: Direct only uses only clean input. Repair local gaps also fills small gaps near known values. Extend daily pattern can also repeat values from 24 hours earlier when enough data already exists.

manual.name: Manual mapping
manual.description: Only applies when **Auto detect** is not selected.

data_description.aggregation_mode: This always applies when multiple source samples land in one planner interval.
data_description.clamp_mode: Nearest interval snaps timestamps to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval. Strict requires exact interval timestamps.
data_description.resample_mode: Linear interpolates between nearby known values. Forward fill repeats the most recent value across short gaps.
data_description.edge_fill_mode: Hold edge repeats the nearest known value at the beginning or end when the source is almost complete.

error.text_too_long: That is too long. Please shorten it.
error.template_required: A template is required for template mode.
error.template_invalid_json: Template output is not valid JSON. Render a JSON list of forecast entries before submitting.
error.adapter_config_required: Entity and adapter type are required for adapter mode.
error.entity_not_found: The selected entity was not found.
error.built_in_requires_energy_kwh: Select an energy sensor with unit `kWh` for the built-in usage forecast.
error.attribute_name_required: An attribute name is required for this adapter.
error.invalid_payload: Source output must be a list of numeric values or a list of objects containing timestamp and value fields.
error.auto_detect_no_match: Auto detect did not find a compatible forecast list. Review the candidate paths and pick manual mapping if needed.
error.auto_detect_conflict: Auto detect found forecast data, but the selected entities did not resolve to the same root path and fields.
error.built_in_no_numeric_history: No usable numeric history was found for the selected load sensor in the configured lookback period.
error.invalid_start: One or more entries has an invalid timestamp. Check the configured time key and use ISO format with timezone.
error.invalid_value: One or more entries has a non-numeric value. Check the configured value key and output numeric values.
error.not_enough_values: The source produced {available_count} usable values for {slot_minutes}-minute intervals, but {required_count} are needed. Try enabling timestamp alignment, aggregation, resampling, or edge fill to produce enough values for the selected horizon.
error.template_invalid_structure: Template returned text. Return a native list of objects or numeric values.
error.source_fetch_error: Source data could not be fetched. Check entity/template settings and try again.
error.name_not_unique: That name is already in use. Please choose a different one.
error.battery_minimum_exceeds_capacity: Set the minimum battery energy below the battery capacity.
error.comfort_target_on_hours_invalid: Set target on-time to be less than or equal to the window duration.
error.comfort_duration_exceeds_window: Set this duration to be less than or equal to the window duration.
error.energy_kwh_required: Please enter the energy needed in kWh.
error.optional_duration_exceeds_window: This run does not fit in the selected run-within window. Set a shorter duration or a longer window.
error.optional_options_exceed_window: These options would overlap in time. Reduce options, shorten duration, lower the minimum gap, or increase the run-within window.

abort.nothing_configured: There is nothing configured in this section yet.

user.title: Add battery
user.description: Create a controllable battery entity for planning. Batteries are assumed to be dispatchable and can be used to shift energy across time, reduce cost, and balance your home profile.

data_description.soc_source: Entity reporting the current state of charge for this battery.
data_description.minimum_kwh: Minimum energy that should remain in the battery. For lithium batteries, a good starting point is about 10% of capacity, for example 1 kWh on a 10 kWh battery.
data_description.max_charge_kw: Maximum charging power used by the planner. A reasonable starting point is about half the battery capacity in kW, for example 5 kW on a 10 kWh battery.
data_description.max_discharge_kw: Maximum discharging power used by the planner. A reasonable starting point is about half the battery capacity in kW, for example 5 kW on a 10 kWh battery.
data_description.can_charge_from_grid: Enable if this battery can charge from the power grid.
data_description.can_charge_from_pv: Enable if this battery can be charged from PV production.

data_description.charge_efficiency: Fraction of charging energy that ends up stored in the battery.
data_description.discharge_efficiency: Fraction of stored energy that can be delivered when discharging.
data_description.prefer_pv_surplus_charging: Recommended for EV batteries or batteries that should generally end up charged. When enabled, any available PV surplus is routed into this battery instead of timing small exports for later recharge.

complete.title: Battery added
complete.description:
**{name}** is now part of this WattPlan setup.

What to do next:
- Create an automation that reads WattPlan's battery action entities and turns them into real battery commands
- Review whether charging from grid and charging from PV match how your battery is wired
- Let WattPlan run so the battery action entities populate with a plan

reconfigure_complete.title: Battery updated
reconfigure_complete.description:
**{name}** has been updated.

What to do next:
- Check that your automation still translates the battery action entities into the right real-world commands
- Let WattPlan run again so the updated battery settings are reflected in the next plan

error.name_not_unique: That name is already in use. Please choose a different one.
error.text_too_long: That is too long. Please shorten it.
error.battery_minimum_exceeds_capacity: Set the minimum battery energy below the battery capacity.
error.battery_efficiency_invalid: Set battery efficiencies to a value greater than 0 and at most 1.

user.title: Add comfort load
user.description: Create a comfort load entity for always-needed systems such as HVAC or pumps. These loads can be deferred, but must still run regularly.

data_description.rolling_window_hours: The rolling period used for on-time accounting. Example: 24 means target on-time is evaluated over the latest 24 hours.
data_description.target_on_hours_per_window: How many hours this load should be on within each rolling window. This must be less than or equal to window duration.
data_description.min_consecutive_on_minutes: Once turned on, keep it on for at least this long. Values are rounded to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval.
data_description.min_consecutive_off_minutes: Once turned off, keep it off for at least this long. Values are rounded to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval.
data_description.max_consecutive_off_minutes: If off for longer than this, planning will force the load back on. Values are rounded to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval.
data_description.on_off_source: Binary sensor used to determine whether the load is currently on or off. WattPlan also uses its history to synthesize runtime context.
data_description.expected_power_kw: Nominal power usage while this load is on.
data_description.measured_power_source: Optional sensor for actual power draw. If provided, future planning can adapt using measured behavior.

data_description.rolling_window_hours: The rolling period used for on-time accounting. Example: 24 means target on-time is evaluated over the latest 24 hours.

data_description.min_consecutive_on_minutes: Once turned on, keep it on for at least this long. Values are rounded to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval.
data_description.min_consecutive_off_minutes: Once turned off, keep it off for at least this long. Values are rounded to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval.
data_description.max_consecutive_off_minutes: If off for longer than this, planning will force the load back on. Values are rounded to the nearest {slot_minutes}-minute interval.

complete.title: Comfort load added
complete.description:
**{name}** is now part of this WattPlan setup.

What to do next:
- Create automations or timers that use WattPlan's comfort action entities to start the load at beneficial times
- Make sure the controlled device can safely be deferred within the window you configured
- Let WattPlan run so the comfort action entities populate with a plan

reconfigure_complete.title: Comfort load updated
reconfigure_complete.description:
**{name}** has been updated.

What to do next:
- Check that your timers or automations still match the updated comfort-load behavior
- Let WattPlan run again so the new timing rules are reflected in the next plan

user.title: Add optional load
user.description: Create an optional load entity for flexible runs such as washers or dryers. The planner returns N start-time options that you can choose from.

data_description.run_within_hours: The planner schedules starts between now and this many hours ahead.
data_description.energy_kwh: Total energy needed for one run.
data_description.options: How many start-time options to return. Each option uses a full duration block and options cannot overlap.
data_description.min_option_gap_minutes: Minimum time between option start times. Larger gaps reduce how many non-overlapping options can fit.

data_description.run_within_hours: The planner schedules starts between now and this many hours ahead.

data_description.options: How many start-time options to return. Each option uses a full duration block and options cannot overlap.
data_description.min_option_gap_minutes: Minimum time between option start times. Larger gaps reduce how many non-overlapping options can fit.

complete.title: Optional load added
complete.description:
**{name}** is now part of this WattPlan setup.

What to do next:
- Show the suggested start times to the people who use this load, for example a washing machine or dryer
- Use WattPlan's optional-load entities to surface the best start windows in a dashboard, notification, or automation
- Let WattPlan run so the start-time options populate with a plan

reconfigure_complete.title: Optional load updated
reconfigure_complete.description:
**{name}** has been updated.

What to do next:
- Check that the way you present the suggested start times still makes sense for the updated timing and energy settings
- Let WattPlan run again so the refreshed options appear in the next plan

source_unavailable.title: {source_title} issue for {entry_title}

source_incomplete.title: {source_title} does not cover the horizon for {entry_title}

abort.entry_removed: The WattPlan setup has been removed

confirm.title: {source_title} does not cover the horizon for {entry_title}
confirm.description:
The configured {source_name} only covers about {covered_hours} hours, but WattPlan needs {required_hours} hours for this setup. This may mean the selected provider is not returning enough forecast data to cover the full planning period.

{grace_note}

**After that, {consequence}**

Submit this repair only if the configured source is expected to contain the right forecast values, but the current setup is too strict. WattPlan will enable these horizon-filling defaults:
- Extend daily pattern
- Nearest timestamp alignment
- Linear gap repair
- Hold edge
